share-of-population-with-large-household:
Share of population with large expenditures on health

The share of the population that spend more than 10% of total household expenditure or income on health.

The choropleth world map is titled for 2021 and uses a sequential scale running from light yellow at 0% through orange to dark red past 35%, with tick marks at every 5%. A hatched swatch marks no data. Two countries stand out in the darkest red at the top of the scale: Angola in southern Africa and Egypt in northeast Africa, both clearly separated from their neighbors in tone. China, together with a band of countries stretching across western and central Asia, is rendered in orange shades placing it toward the upper part of the scale. Parts of southeastern Europe and the southern Caucasus also show orange tones. Most of South America, including the large landmass of Brazil, sits in pale yellow at the low end of the scale, as do Canada, the United States, Mexico, Australia, the Nordic countries, and much of western Europe. India and several of its neighbors take on a mid-range yellow-orange tint. Large hatched regions covering Greenland, much of sub-Saharan Africa, and several countries in Southeast Asia and the Pacific mark the absence of data. The darkest reds are confined to isolated countries rather than forming continuous blocks, while the pale low-end shading dominates the Americas, Oceania, and western Europe. The color gradient progresses continuously from yellow through orange to red, and the map title cites 2021 as the reference year.
liberal-political-institutions-index:
Liberal Political Institutions Index

Data by V-Dem. Expert estimates of the extent of individual and minority rights, equality before the law, and constraints on the executive by the legislature and courts. The index ranges from 0 to 1 (most liberal).

The choropleth world map is labeled 2025 and uses a sequential color ramp running from pale yellow at 0 through light green and teal to deep navy at 1, with tick marks at every 0.1 step. A hatched swatch denotes no data. Dark navy shading covering the upper part of the scale spans Canada and the United States in North America, most of western and northern Europe, Australia, New Zealand, Japan, and, in South America, a large dark-blue block covering Brazil, Argentina, Uruguay, and Chile. South Africa in southern Africa also sits in a deep blue tone. Mid-range teal and blue-green shades cover much of sub-Saharan Africa, parts of Southeast Asia including Indonesia, India on the South Asian subcontinent, and Mexico. The lowest end of the scale, in pale yellow and light green, runs in a broad belt across North Africa and the Middle East through Central Asia into China and Mongolia, and also covers Russia, which forms the largest pale expanse on the map. A hatched patch appears over Greenland to indicate missing data. The deepest values cluster in the Americas, western Europe, and Oceania, while the palest values form a continuous band through North Africa, the Middle East, Central Asia, and northern Eurasia.
lgbt-legal-equality-index:
LGBT+ Legal Equality Index

The index captures to which extent LGBT+ people have the same rights as straight and cisgender people. It combines 15 individual policies, such as the legality of same-sex relationships, marriage, and gender marker changes. It ranges from 0 to 100 (most equal).

The choropleth world map is labeled 2025 and uses a diverging color ramp running from deep red at 0 through orange and yellow through green and teal to purple at 100, with tick marks every 10. A hatched swatch denotes no data. The deepest purple at the top end of the scale covers most of western Europe, including Spain, France, the United Kingdom, Germany, and the Nordic countries, along with a block covering Argentina, Chile, and Uruguay in southern South America, plus New Zealand. Canada, Brazil, Colombia, and Australia sit in teal and blue tones in the upper-middle part of the scale. The United States is rendered in a green shade somewhat below that band. Mexico, much of Central America, and southern African countries including South Africa fall in yellow-green mid-range tones. The deep red lowest band covers a contiguous belt across North Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, Iran, and much of central and eastern Africa, with additional red patches in Southeast Asia including Malaysia. Russia and China sit in orange shades low on the scale. India and Thailand take on yellow-green mid-range hues. Greenland and a handful of smaller states are hatched. The color pattern places the upper end of the scale in western Europe, southern South America, and Oceania, while the lower end forms a broad band across North Africa, the Middle East, and central Africa.
share-of-the-population-living-in-extrem:
Share of population living in extreme poverty vs. mean income or consumption per day

Extreme poverty is defined as living below the International Poverty Line of $3 per day. This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

The scatter plot is labeled 2025 and plots the share of the population in extreme poverty on the vertical axis, marked from 0% to above 80%, against average annual income or consumption in international dollars at 2021 prices on a logarithmic horizontal axis with gridlines at $1,000, $2,000, $5,000, $10,000, and $20,000. Each circle is a country, sized by population, and colored by region: North America in salmon, South America in dark red, Africa in purple, Europe in grey-blue, Asia in teal, and Oceania in light blue. A legend shows reference circles for 1.4 billion and 600 million. DR Congo sits at the top left near $1,000 with a share above 80%. Madagascar lies just to the right at roughly 70% to 75%, and Niger sits near 60%. Nigeria and Mali form a cluster around 40% near the $2,000 gridline. Pakistan, Syria, and Senegal sit between 15% and 25% near $2,000. South Africa is labeled around 17% to the right of this group. India, shown as a very large teal circle, sits near $2,000 with a share near 5%. Indonesia, Iran, China, Brazil, Turkey, and the United States stretch along the horizontal axis from roughly $4,000 to beyond $20,000, all with shares at or near 0%. The note states that values are in international dollars at 2021 prices and relate to income after taxes and benefits or to consumption, per capita, depending on country and year. The overall pattern descends from high shares at low incomes to near-zero shares at high incomes.
percentage-of-primary-schools-with-toile:
Share of primary schools with toilets

Share of public primary schools equipped with with separate girls and boys sanitation facilities or toilets on the location of the educational institution.

The choropleth world map is labeled 2024 and uses a sequential scale running from dark red at 0% through orange and yellow to pale cream at 100%, with tick marks at every 10% step. A hatched swatch denotes no data. Sanitation facilities or toilets include a pit latrine, an improved pit latrine, a flush toilet, a pour-flush toilet, or a composting toilet. Deep red shading at the low end of the scale is visible in a cluster of countries across sub-Saharan Africa, including Mali, Niger, Chad, Ethiopia, and Madagascar, as well as Pakistan in South Asia and a large block covering Indonesia and the Philippines in Southeast Asia. Surrounding African countries take on orange mid-range tones. Mexico and several small central American states sit in orange as well. India, much of northern Africa, Iran, and a number of south-eastern European countries are rendered in pale yellow shades toward the high end of the scale. The United States, Canada, most of western Europe, Japan, and Australia sit in the palest cream at the top of the scale. Large hatched regions cover Greenland, Russia, China, most of South America including Brazil and Argentina, and substantial parts of central Africa and the Middle East, marking absent data. The lowest values form discrete patches across sub-Saharan Africa and maritime Southeast Asia, while the highest values dominate North America, western Europe, and Oceania.
cumulative-number-artificial-intelligenc:
Cumulative AI-related bills passed into law since 2016, as of 2024

Bills passed into law by national legislative bodies (e.g., congress, parliament) with the keyword "artificial intelligence" (translated to the respective languages) in the title or body of the bill.

The choropleth world map covers the period 2016 through 2024 and uses a discrete blue scale with six categories: no data shown by hatching, 0, 1, 2, 5 to 10, and 10 or more, arranged from palest to darkest blue. The United States and Russia are shaded in the darkest blue, placing them in the 10-or-more category; the note records the Identifying Outputs of Generative Adversarial Networks Act as an example passed into law by the US Congress. France also sits in the darkest band. A mid-blue shade corresponding to the 5 to 10 group covers the United Kingdom, Italy, Germany, Poland, Ukraine, South Korea, Japan, China, and Argentina. Canada, Spain, India, and a scattering of European and Asian states take on the lighter 2 or 1 shades. Large portions of the map, including most of Africa, the Middle East, Central Asia, and much of South America and Southeast Asia, are marked with diagonal hatching to denote missing data. A few countries, including Mexico, Brazil, Australia, and several African and Southeast Asian states with solid pale fills, sit in the 0 band. The concentration of the darker categories lies in North America, Europe, and northeast Asia, while the hatched no-data swathe dominates the tropics and the southern continents outside the named cases.
key-features-of-human-rights:
Key features of human rights

The indices are by V-Dem, based on expert estimates, and range from 0 to 1 (most rights). Physical integrity includes freedom from government torture and political killings. Private civil liberties include property rights and freedom of movement, religion, and from forced labor. Political civil liberties include freedom of expression and association.

The figure shows four line charts for Brazil, each plotting a single blue line from 1789 to 2025 on a vertical axis running from 0 to 1. In the upper-left panel, "Human rights overall" starts near 0.15, steps up to about 0.35 around 1830, fluctuates in the 0.3 to 0.45 range through the late 1800s, rises around 1890 toward 0.5, dips and oscillates through the first half of the twentieth century, drops near 1970, then climbs steeply after 1980 to a plateau above 0.8, with a recent dip and partial recovery ending near 0.85. The upper-right "Physical integrity" panel holds near 0.2 until the late 1800s, rises in steps to around 0.45 by 1930, falls to near 0 around 1970, then rises after 1980 to about 0.7, dips briefly, and ends near 0.8. The lower-left "Private civil liberties" panel starts near 0.15, steps up to about 0.55 around 1890, plateaus near 0.7 through the mid-1900s, climbs after 1980 to about 0.9, and closes near that level. The lower-right "Political civil liberties" panel starts near 0, jumps to about 0.7 around 1830, drops near 1890, falls close to 0 around 1970, rises rapidly after 1980 to a peak above 0.9, and closes at that level.
